topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in Professor Albright’s office felt suddenly thin, devoid of the familiar, comforting weight of his presence. When he announced his departure, a strange, sharp feeling tightened in my chest. It wasn't sadness, not exactly; it was a sudden, exhilarating opening. I watched him pack his desk, his movements efficient, almost rehearsed. A flicker of something sharp and calculating sparked behind my eyes. This was it. The path was clearing. I immediately began mentally cataloging every piece of unpublished data he possessed, every confidential note he’d ever shared, seeing them not as academic property, but as leverage. I leaned forward, my fingers drumming a restless rhythm on the mahogany surface. "Of course, Professor," I said, my voice smooth, carrying an undertone of practiced deference that masked the churning ambition beneath. "I'll need access to the full archive for my final chapter."

[story 2]

I stared at the notice pinned to the departmental bulletin board. Albright was leaving. The silence in my dorm room amplified the sound of my own pulse. A warm, insistent pressure built low in my stomach, a feeling of immense opportunity knocking loudly on my door. I didn't bother with the usual anxious pacing. Instead, I pulled out my laptop, the screen illuminating my face with a determined, almost hungry expression. I started drafting contingency plans, mapping out how I could intercept the research Albright was currently compiling. Every successful publication, every coveted grant—it suddenly seemed within reach, contingent only on my swift, strategic maneuvering. I picked up my phone, not to call a friend, but to look up the administrative codes for restricted university files. A slow, satisfied smile spread across my lips as I began to organize my strategy.

[story 3]

The news hit me like a physical jolt. Professor Albright was gone. My initial reaction wasn't sorrow, but a sudden, intense focus, a narrowing of my entire world down to a single, glittering objective. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, a deliberate disruption in the quiet space. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the tension radiating outward. I needed to be the one who benefited from this transition. I walked to the door, pausing only to look back at the empty space where his imposing figure had been. "This changes things," I murmured to the empty room, the words tasting metallic and sharp on my tongue. I felt a profound sense of entitlement, as if the entire academic landscape was suddenly shifting into a terrain I was uniquely positioned to conquer. I started drafting an email, carefully worded, suggesting a collaboration that was far more beneficial to me than any standard student interaction.
